content:
I once spent three weeks obsessing over a single prompt for a customer support chatbot.  

I tweaked tone, added few-shot examples, even tried chain-of-thought. The output *felt* better. My team was impressed.  

Then we ran a blind evaluation against our old version.  

The result? No statistically significant improvement. Worse — the new prompt hallucinated more on edge cases.  

That was my wake-up call.  

We were building with AI the way people built with magic: hope, intuition, and a lot of fiddling.  

Now I practice **evaluation-driven development** (EDD). It’s the closest thing we have to test-driven development for LLMs — except you can’t just assert `response == expected`.  

Here’s the contrarian bit: most teams over-invest in prompt engineering and RAG pipelines before they have a single reliable eval metric.  

They optimise what they can see — the prompt, the retrieval, the temperature — instead of what they can measure — precision, recall, toxicity, faithfulness.  

But LLMs are stochastic. A prompt that works today might fail tomorrow. An eval suite is the only thing that keeps you honest.  

I’ve learned three things the hard way:  

1. **Start with a golden dataset.** Even 50 hand-labeled examples are better than nothing. You need ground truth for your specific use case — not a generic benchmark.  

2. **Eval as you build, not after.** Hook automated evals into your CI/CD pipeline. If a new prompt drops accuracy by 2%, you catch it before shipping.  

3. **Use multiple signals.** One metric is a liar. Pair semantic similarity with factuality checks. Add user feedback if possible.  

The biggest lesson? EDD doesn’t just make your app better — it makes your team faster. You stop debating opinions and start debating data.  

“This prompt feels more helpful” becomes “This prompt scores 0.87 on correctness vs 0.79.”  

AI is unpredictable. Your development process shouldn’t be.  

What’s the hardest eval challenge you’ve faced building with LLMs?  

#EvaluationDrivenDevelopment #LLM #BestPractices
